hypothesis: After primary oracle convergence (violators=[]), add capacity to ticks with borderline response times (within borderline_margin_s of SLA). These ticks are most vulnerable to stochastic spot interruptions.
research_motivation: OSOTSS misses AMCSG by 15 requests on BurstGPT (n_sla_safe=5849 vs 5864). Root cause: oracle terminates at violators=[] but 15 borderline requests fail under stochastic spot interruptions. SSM run (PR #62) confirmed interrupt_safety_margin is ineffective (secondary violators=[] break fires before primary convergence check). OSSC adds a post-convergence phase specifically targeting borderline-tick capacity.

same_conditions.trace: same (Azure LLM 2024 5880 req, BurstGPT HF 5880 req)
same_conditions.decision_time_info: same (no oracle token access in planning)

key_findings: OSSC narrows BurstGPT gap from -15 to -3 requests at margin=5.0s, but never fully closes it
Every positive margin reduces Azure goodput/$ monotonically (-0.66% at 0.5s, -5.31% at 5.0s)
All margins maintain Azure n_sla_safe=5823 (no SLA regression on Azure)
BurstGPT n_sla_safe improves by at most +12 requests (5849→5861 at 5.0s), still -3 vs AMCSG
No margin achieves both: goodput/$>=baseline AND n_sla_safe>=AMCSG on both traces
Root cause: borderline ticks accumulate extra capacity cost that hurts goodput/$ more than the SLA improvement helps
root_cause: OSSC adds capacity to borderline ticks (response time in (SLA-margin, SLA]) after primary convergence. These capacity additions are real costs that reduce goodput/$. On BurstGPT, the 15 missing requests span ticks where stochastic interruptions reduce c_effective, and adding +1 deterministic capacity to those ticks does not fully compensate because some borderline requests span multiple ticks and the SLA gap is driven by capacity reduction, not response time proximity.
infrastructure_retained: borderline_margin_s=0.0 default is byte-identical to pre-OSSC behavior
five_failure_rule: TRIGGERED. 5 consecutive non-frontier-improvement runs: C1PGS (run 1), SOTSS-GSF (run 2), Adaptive EWMA (run 3), Stochastic Safety Margin (run 4), OSSC/Borderline (run 5). Focus rule: stop adding modules; focus exclusively on integrating existing modules, replay validation, benchmark realism, bottleneck diagnosis, architecture simplification.
